messages.content:
Help me to draft a response to this email from my customer, Billy Holmes: "Hello Sunday,

 

Thank you again for all of your help on getting the draft air permit written, I greatly appreciate it!!

 

I wanted to circle back on a conversation we had early on in the permitting process regarding a concern brought up by Jonathan Brickey and the recent non-attainment reclassification for NOx in our area. I know I shared this earlier in the permitting process, but at the time we were also navigating modeling, which we took care of, so I wanted bring back up the follow up response I had to Jonathan but never received feedback on. Darling wants to avoid becoming a Title V facility and we believe that we are not above the non-attainment threshold. To support that, Darling has provided the following to Jonathan.

 

The first thing to note is that Darling owns two separate properties there in Denver. There is the main rendering plant (Darling Ingredients Inc.), which is where this replacement boiler is going, and all of the permits are listed under the 93AD1629 number. The second site is the edible food plant (Darling Ingredients Inc. – DBA Pepcol Manufacturing), and those permits are under the 99AD0779 designation. Darling considers those to be two completely separate facilities and to our knowledge the APCD has generally accepted that. The two facilities do not share any common processes, plans, or permits; they also have two different SIC codes. The 18.0 tpy of NOx emissions associated with those grandfathered boilers are for the Pepcol location. If we are able to maintain those as two separate facilities then that would keep us below the non-attainment threshold for NOx.

 

Do you know if CDPHE is considering the two facilities as a single source? If so, do you think you could bring our concerns up to Jonathan as he has not responded to any phone calls or emails since initially notifying us? As I mentioned earlier, Darling does not want to become a Title V facility as our two sites are not connected and the actual emissions from the site are well below the updated non-attainment threshold.

 

Thank you,

 

Billy Holmes"

Dear <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>,

Thank you for reaching out and for your kind words. I appreciate your continued trust in my assistance with the draft air permit.

Regarding the concern raised by Jonathan Brickey and the recent non-attainment reclassification for NOx in your area, I understand that Darling wants to avoid becoming a Title V facility. I remember discussing this issue earlier in the permitting process and acknowledging the need to address it.

Darling has provided information to Jonathan to support their claim that their two properties in Denver, the rendering plant (Darling Ingredients Inc.) and the edible food plant (Darling Ingredients Inc. - DBA Pepcol Manufacturing), should be considered separate facilities. These properties have distinct permits, processes, plans, and SIC codes. Additionally, the 18.0 tpy of NOx emissions associated with the grandfathered boilers pertain only to the Pepcol location. By maintaining these facilities as separate entities, Darling believes they can remain below the non-attainment threshold for NOx.

I understand your concern regarding Jonathan's lack of response to your previous communication attempts. Rest assured, I will take proactive steps to address this situation. I will reach out to Jonathan and bring up your concerns about the potential categorization of the two facilities as a single source. My aim is to ensure that Darling's position is adequately communicated and that any relevant updates or feedback are promptly shared with you.

Thank you for bringing this matter to my attention, and rest assured I will do my best to assist you in resolving this concern. If you have any further questions or if there's anything else I can do to support you, please don't hesitate to let me know.

Best regards,

Sunday

Thank you for reaching out and for your kind words. I appreciate your continued trust in my assistance with the draft air permit.

Regarding the concern raised by Jonathan Brickey and the recent non-attainment reclassification for NOx in your area, I understand that Darling wants to avoid becoming a Title V facility. I remember discussing this issue earlier in the permitting process and acknowledging the need to address it.

I have copied Johnathan on this email and will discuss the issue with him. I will get back to you after discussing it with Jonathan.

Thank you for bringing this matter to my attention, and rest assured I will do my best to help you resolve this concern. If you have any further questions or if there’s anything else I can do to support you, please don’t hesitate to let me know.

Best regards,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>, PE, DM."

Dear <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>,

Thank you for reaching out and for your kind words. I appreciate your continued trust in my assistance with the draft air permit.

Regarding the concern raised by Jonathan Brickey and the recent non-attainment reclassification for NOx in your area, I understand that Darling wants to avoid becoming a Title V facility. We have previously discussed this issue during the permitting process, and it was acknowledged that it needed to be addressed.

Darling has provided information to Jonathan to support their claim that their two properties in Denver, the rendering plant (Darling Ingredients Inc.) and the edible food plant (Darling Ingredients Inc. - DBA Pepcol Manufacturing), should be considered separate facilities. These properties have distinct permits, processes, plans, and SIC codes. Moreover, the 18.0 tpy of NOx emissions associated with the grandfathered boilers are specific to the Pepcol location. By treating these facilities as separate entities, Darling believes they can remain below the non-attainment threshold for NOx.

I understand your concern about Jonathan’s lack of response to your previous communication attempts. I assure you that I will take proactive steps to address this situation. I will reach out to Jonathan and communicate your concerns about categorizing the two facilities as a single source. My aim is to ensure that Darling’s position is adequately conveyed, and that any relevant updates or feedback are promptly shared with you.

In fact, I have copied Jonathan on this email to ensure that all parties are included in the discussion. Following my conversation with Jonathan, I will provide you with further information and updates.

Thank you for bringing this matter to my attention, and please rest assured that I will do my best to help you resolve this concern. If you have any further questions or if there’s anything else I can do to support you, please don’t hesitate to let me know.

Best regards,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>, PE, DM.

Help draft a response to this email: "Samantha and/or Sunday,

 

I hope all is well. I tried to find a copy of the draft construction permit in the links that were provided with your email below but did not see it anywhere. Please provide a copy for review as soon as you are able..

 

Thanks,

Debra"

Dear Debra,

I hope this email finds you well. I apologize for the inconvenience you experienced in locating the draft construction permit in the links provided. I understand the importance of reviewing the document promptly. 

Allow me to rectify the situation. I will promptly send you a copy of the draft construction permit for your review. I apologize for any inconvenience this may have caused, and I appreciate your patience.

Thank you for bringing this matter to my attention, and please do not hesitate to reach out if you have any further questions or if there's anything else I can assist you with. 

Best regards,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>

Help review this email for clarity: "Dear Virtue,

Good morning. I hope that you are doing well.

Yes, I need your sign-off on the draft permits to let me know that the drafts do not contain any errors.
Thanks and God bless,"

Dear Virtue,

I hope this email finds you well and that you are having a good morning.

I am reaching out to request your sign-off on the draft permits. This sign-off will serve as confirmation that you have reviewed the drafts and have not identified any errors or discrepancies.

Thank you in advance for your assistance in this matter. May you have a blessed day.

Best regards,
What are the EPA's three factors for determining a single source?

The EPA, or Environmental Protection Agency, uses three factors to determine whether multiple emissions sources should be considered a single source for regulatory purposes. These factors are often referred to as the "common sense" factors. They include:

1. Common Control: The degree of common control between the sources, such as common ownership, operations, management, or financial arrangements.

2. Common Purpose: The extent to which the sources are engaged in the same industrial activity, have the same primary function, or are part of a common production process.

3. Common Emissions: The degree to which the sources emit similar pollutants, have similar emission rates, use similar control devices, or discharge emissions to a common location.

These factors help the EPA determine whether sources should be considered a single entity or separate entities when it comes to regulatory requirements, such as permitting, reporting, and compliance standards.
what is SCIC code?

I believe you meant to ask about the SIC code, not SCIC code. SIC stands for Standard Industrial Classification. It is a system developed by the U.S. government to classify and identify different industries and sectors of the economy. Each industry is assigned a unique SIC code, which is a numerical code ranging from two to four digits.

SIC codes are used for various purposes, such as economic analysis, statistical reporting, and regulatory compliance. They help categorize businesses based on their primary economic activity, allowing for easier comparison and analysis of data within specific industries or sectors.

While the SIC code system has been largely replaced by the North American Industry Classification System (NAICS), some organizations, regulatory agencies, and databases still use SIC codes for certain purposes.

Please, review this email message for clarity: "Dear <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>,

Again, thank you for reaching out and for your kind words. Regarding the concern raised by Jonathan Brickey, I have taken a quick review of these points, I am inclined to consider both points 009 (two boilers) and point 011 (a boiler) as a single point. I do not know the information you gave to Jonathan to support their claim that the two sources may not be a single point for air permitting purposes. However, based on the EPA's 3-part test for a single emission source, namely (1) Common Control, (2) Adjacent or Contiguous location, and (3) Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) Code or Common Pollutant.

The test for a single consideration:

1. Both points are owned and controlled by the same or common owner, Darling Ingredients Inc.,
2. Both points are co-located, adjacent or contiguous to each other, and 
3. Both points emit a common pollutant, NOx, or belong to the same SIC code.

Therefore, based on the information available to me, it appears that the three conditions for being a single emission source are met. Unless you have incontrovertible information that negates any of the three points above, the ongoing permit process will assume both points as a single source. As such, permit 23AD0268 processing and issuance will be on hold pending your response to this email.

Therefore, I request that you submit such incontrovertible evidence to me promptly.

Best regards,


<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>, PE, DM."

Dear <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>,

Thank you again for reaching out and for your kind words. Regarding the concern raised by Jonathan Brickey, I have reviewed the points you mentioned. Based on the information available to me, I am inclined to consider both points 009 (two boilers) and point 011 (a boiler) as a single point. Currently, I do not have the information you provided to Jonathan to support the claim that these sources may not be considered a single point for air permitting purposes.

When determining whether multiple sources should be considered a single emission source, the EPA applies a three-part test. This test involves considering common control, adjacent or contiguous location, and the Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) Code or common pollutant.

In this case, the information I have suggests that the three conditions for being a single emission source are met. Both points are owned and controlled by the same owner, Darling Ingredients Inc. Additionally, they are co-located and emit a common pollutant, NOx, or belong to the same SIC code.

Therefore, unless you can provide conclusive evidence that contradicts any of the three points above, we will proceed with assuming both points as a single source in the ongoing permit process. Consequently, the processing and issuance of permit 23AD0268 will be put on hold until we receive your response to this email.

I kindly request that you promptly submit any evidence that could invalidate the points mentioned above.

Best regards,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>, PE, DM.

Review this for clarity: "Dear <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>,

Thanks for your understanding.  The best approach will be to process both sources together since they constitute a single source. Therefore, I will advise that you submit a revised APEN to modify 99AD0779 to limit its emissions so that NOx emissions from both points will be less than 25 tons per year. In addition, include a cover letter it explaining the background situation and request expedited processing. Also, request that the application be assigned to me since I am already working on the other permit so that the emissions from both points together. I am more than willing to assist in the process and process the applications in a timely manner. 
Thanks and God bless,"

Dear <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>,

Thank you for your understanding. To address the situation effectively, it would be best to process both sources as a single source. I recommend that you submit a revised APEN (Air Pollution Emission Notice) to modify permit 99AD0779, limiting its emissions so that the combined NOx emissions from both points will be under 25 tons per year.

To expedite the processing of your application, I advise including a cover letter that explains the background situation and explicitly requests expedited processing. Additionally, please request that the application be assigned to me, as I am already working on the other permit. This will help ensure that the emissions from both points are considered together in the process.

Rest assured, I am committed to assisting you and processing the applications in a timely manner. If you have any further questions or require any additional guidance, please do not hesitate to reach out.

Thank you, and may you be blessed.

Best regards,

Help review this for clarity: "Hi KC,

Good evening. I am not sure what happened to the draft permit. I will have to rewrite it. PTS indicated that an IA was issued on 9/17/2009, and another IA was issued on 6/25/2020. I will double-check this and let you know when it is ready.. 
Thanks and God bless,"

Hi KC,

I hope this email finds you well. I apologize for the inconvenience, but it seems that the draft permit has gone missing. I will need to rewrite it. According to PTS, an Initial Assessment (IA) was issued on 9/17/2009, and another IA was issued on 6/25/2020. I will verify this information and inform you as soon as the rewritten draft permit is ready.

Thank you for your understanding, and if you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to let me know.

Best regards,

Help review this email to me supervisor for clarity and appropriateness: "Hi KC,
Good evening again. Thanks for your response on PME scoring. So you rated me 2 out of five for not knowing that the has been a redesignation and counted that as technical knowledge? You have seen two errors in hundreds of permits written. I will recall two instances where you were wrong on technical understanding, namely (1) when you said that I was wrong when I processed a gas station was a true minor. You only agreed with me after you made consultations. As a result of this type of misclassification, many sources have been wrongly processed as synthetic minor instead of being true minor permits and (2) you are questioning that I exempt some emergency generators under the specific permit exemption under Regulation Number 3.B.II.D.1.c.(iii): "Have uncontrolled actual emissions are less than five tons per year (Regulation Number 3.B.II.D.1.c.(iii))."
I noticed that many minor sources have been wrongly classified as synthetic minor permits, making many customers spend money and time on unnecessary permit modifications. The question will be: is it fair to rate you 2 out of 5 just because of these errors?
Please, rerate my performance ratings and let me know if you will do this or not.Thanks and God bless,
 Sunday A. Fadeyi, PE, DM."

Hi KC,

Good evening again. Thank you for your response regarding the PME scoring. I would like to discuss the rating you gave me for not knowing about the redesignation and counting it as a lack of technical knowledge. I understand that you have noticed two errors among the many permits I have worked on. However, I would like to highlight two instances where I believe you had a misunderstanding of technical concepts:

1. When I processed a gas station as a true minor source, you initially disagreed with me. It was only after consulting with others that you agreed with my assessment. This type of misclassification has resulted in many sources being incorrectly processed as synthetic minor permits instead of true minor permits.

2. You are questioning my decision to exempt certain emergency generators under the specific permit exemption outlined in Regulation Number 3.B.II.D.1.c.(iii): "Have uncontrolled actual emissions of less than five tons per year." I believe these generators meet the criteria for exemption.

I have observed that many minor sources have been improperly classified as synthetic minor permits, causing unnecessary expenses and delays for our customers. Given these circumstances, I would like to discuss the fairness of rating me 2 out of 5 based on these errors.

I kindly request that you reconsider my performance rating and provide me with your decision. I appreciate your attention to this matter.

Thank you, and may you be blessed.

Sunday A. Fadeyi, PE, DM.
